speaker: interviewer
text: Today is April 1, 1996. The survivor is Mania Tisch Lichtenstein. The interviewer is Joseph Bobrow in Scottsdale, Arizona. The country is the United States of America. The language is English. My name is Joseph Bobrow. Today's date is April 1, 1996. I am conducting an interview with survivor Mania Lichtenstein. The interview is being conducted in Scottsdale, Arizona in the United States of America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your complete name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Mania Lichtenstein, born Tisch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-N-I-A L-I-C-H-T-E-N-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the name at birth? No, the name of birth was Tisch. T-I-C-- S, I'm sorry-- S-C-H. What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday is, is March 5, 1923, as I was registered during the war. But my real birthday is December 26, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Poland. The name of the city is Wlodzimierz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-L-O-D-Z-I-M-I-E-R-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the hometown when you were a child growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a mediocre city. It wasn't a large city, but yet not small. Very cultural, as for the times in Poland, comparing to, you know-- considering the times. There were so many public schools. We had two gymnasiums. We had an agricultural school-- which was a lot for those days in Poland. And life was very, very good. I mean, the Jews were always persecuted. The anti-Semitism existed, but we didn't know anything else, so there was no complaint about it. You just lived your life, and everything was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next to what large city was it located?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was next to Lodz-- Lwow, in Polish. I'm sorry. I'm sorry. Can we erase it? Lodz-- no, it's not Lodz. It's Lwow. And in German it's Lemberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know about how far the distance was between this large town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I really don't know, but it wasn't next to-- very close, you know. But this was-- it was in the same province.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your address, where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was-- the street was [POLISH] 13, Wlodzimierz, Wolynski. Wolyn was like a province or a state.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your house was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it, it-- ours was quite a nice house because people lived very primitively, then. Whatever was inherited from your grandparents or-- We had a little foyer, then we had a huge kitchen. And then we had a study where we girls used to do our homework. And we had a bathroom, and we had that we used to call-- my mother used to call salon, the salon where it was off-limits for the kids-- only for very, very special occasions, you know. Was a beautiful room with flowers and it was off-limits.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have your own room-- your own bedroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that would have been a too-big luxury for those days. No, I slept with my sister in a-- in a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: The town itself-- can you give us an idea how large a population it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll just say what-- to my knowledge-- what I've heard, that it was about 26,000 Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about the total population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews. I don't know. I really don't-- never bothered knowing, you know? Didn't know it will be important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Jews a minority in the city--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or was it mainly Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mainly Jewish. Not mainly, but-- yeah-- majority Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: More than half.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Poles, and then peasants lived more on the outskirts of town, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the Jewish community-- the synagogues, the institutions, the-- did they have a theater?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was quite young, but whatever I perceived or remember hearing-- it was-- it was a good life. It didn't have a the-- it had one movie theater, which was a big, big event, to go to the movies. You needed money to go to the movies, and the most people weren't too well off. Excuse me. But I remember the Jewish theater was a very important aspect. And whenever-- and, and there was a such a thing existed in Poland. It was very, very prominent, the Jewish theater, you know, the Jewish culture. And so, whenever a Jewish theater came to town, of course the whole town went to see it. And I remember seeing a few-- my mother would take me, maybe because I was the youngest and she didn't want to leave me alone. But I remember very, very distinctly remember, because the music-- I remember some of them, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, anything with music and songs and theater. Exciting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there-- was there a synagogue in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was one synagogue. There were many, as they called them, shtiebel, which was like little, you know, private congregations, little ones. But there was one main synagogue. And this is where even, even from our schools, like when they were gathering for some holiday, the gatherings were taken place in the synagogue. It was like a monument. Very important-- very important entity in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who the rabbi was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the rabbi's name was Morgensztern Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your parents. What was-- what were their names, first?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents was Gerszon and Gitel Tisch. My father-- they-- at, at-- they were there-- oh, sorry. There-- I'll try to remember as much as possible. Do you want to know their ages?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, well, tell me what your father did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was he like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a drugstore. And he was like a father, you know? [LAUGHS] I don't know. I can't say anything specific.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was very heartwarming for me, that I remember-- my father, again, was a modern man. And the drugstore was not, like, next door, so you had to commute. And there was no, no bus or such things. But he used to go on a bicycle, which made him very modern, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would-- every night, when my father would come from work, I would meet him on the corner, because before we would head home, he'd take me to the sh-- [POLISH], where they-- where they-- sweet shop. Because I had a-- still have a sweet tooth. [LAUGHS] And I-- and he would buy me something. So this was so memorable. Every night, I would get some candies or, you know, chocolate. This, my father. Also what's memorable-- my father would go with me and make-- made to-- custom-made sled. You know, those were times that you couldn't go in the store and buy whatever you wanted. Everything was made home-- handmade or, you know, custom-made. And that sled, I'll never forget. It was the envy of the whole street because some kids would even slide down the hill on a piece of cardboard-- anything that would take you down. But I had a real sled made up-- custom-made. Was very proud of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and my mother was a very caring. I'm just sorry I-- I could not perceive it then because I was too young. It was-- oh, mother. So what else is new? A mother is there for you. But now, later on, when I have lost her and when I matured, I really-- I would cry-- ask her for forgiveness that why I didn't appreciate her more, you know? Show her-- because, as I'll relate later, I thought she was such a hero. You know? She was a typical Jewish mother-- caring for her kids and the-- more than-- cared about our well-being above her own. Do you have any special memories of her-- of doing anything together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [LAUGHS] [SIGHS] As I said, I still have a sweet tooth. And every Thursday, my mother would bake for Shabbos. And, of course, if there is baking and there's going to be baking of cookies and cake, I am not going to sleep. And next day, there is school. You have to go to sleep. And I was still very, very young, you know? I had to go to sleep early. I'm not going to sleep because she's baking cookies. And every, every Thursday night, my mother would solemnly promise, as soon as the cookies come out of the oven, I'm going to wake you up. And in those days, you were allowed to lie to your children. [LAUGHS] Now, you can't do it. So every Friday morning I would come storming in the-- you didn't wake me up! You didn't wake me! You said you'll wake me! But she--

speaker: interviewer
text: That happened, like, every Thursday. How can I go to sleep, when the cookies will soon be out of the oven? So, this. Or she used to-- my mother was quite like a modern, for those times. Pretty lady, and very elegant, and she cared how she looked. And she knew all the modern dances.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember she would-- while we were waiting-- like, while I was-- would always assist her during the baking. That, you cannot get me away. And she would show me the modern dance. I forgot how it-- she told me the name. And in the kitchen floor-- on the kitchen floor we would dance-- I still remember the step, and I remember the melody.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the melody go? Can you tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING] I remember the-- something like this. You know, it was the modern dance, in those days. Not many women, in those days, bothered about dancing and modern dances, or-- but my mother liked everything modern. Used to call her a coquette, a coquette.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father-- can you give us a physical description of him? Was he tall, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think anybody was real tall. And if he was, he was outstanding-- it wasn't nice to be tall. [LAUGHS] My father-- medium, medium. And he was slender. He used to smoke, which was, in those days, nothing to it. The men smoked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cigarettes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cigarettes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have a beard, or mustache?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. My father was also a modern man. Like most Jews were dressed in kapote, you know, in that coat, or even had sideburn-- the peyes. No, my father was modern, shaved. Not many shaved. And he wore a Derby hat, which already signified a modern man, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages were spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home, it was spoken Yiddish-- Jewish. But we, the new generation, spoke Polish, even only if it's-- we spoke Polish, and also for the reason to-- we were always afraid. Like, we never walked freely in the street, giggling like young kids. Giggling, laughing, you know-- free. Always looked over the shoulders, because we were so persecuted by the Polish kids. They would spit at us. They would throw stones.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you always-- and you were always cautious. Don't rock the boat. Don't speak loud. Don't-- it's amazing. I guess it still remains. You would see it in our mentality. Like, always intimidated, like-- shh, don't talk loud. Don't do this. This was a very-- I mean, I was young, but I kind of consider myself-- [LAUGHS] philosophical, [LAUGHS] like, analystic, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember-- so we would speak-- let's say I'll walk with my little girlfriend. We'll speak Polish, kind of-- shh, quiet, don't talk Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about any brothers or sisters that you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, God. Because just mentioning sisters, I'm ready to cry. [SIGHS] I always think that having a sister makes you so important, like you belong somewhere. You know, you're not just coming from nowhere. I had one sister. Her name was Rifka. She liked to call herself in Polish-- we all had Polish names-- Regina. And she really was my idol, it's true. Like, she-- next to God, she was-- everything she did, I had to-- I, I had to ape her. I would do-- and I did, in my life. Everything that she would do, I tried to copy. And I had another sister, [? Nehamke, ?] which was Nechama. She was like my playmate because she was only two and a half years older than I, so we played together. But she was a very-- we used to say that the house is burning down, and she would sit in the corner, very studious, and read. Let the house burn down. She's not moving. She's reading. So but she was my-- actually, because only two and a half years older, so we played together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the age difference between you and Rifka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that would have been eight and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was older than you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I looked up the her. To me, she was-- she was educated. She  was smart. She was-- she, she, she really was my idol. And I had to do-- everything that she did in her life, I had to follow in her steps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides those two sisters, did you have any other brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had a little brother who died a long time before I was born. And only what I've heard in the home, that he was five years old when he died of a bowel-- twist of bowels, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Meyer, and he died at age five. It must have been a tragedy beyond description for my mother. The first one, and a boy. It was very important, you know? And a kaddish, as they called it. And, you know, five years old, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any grandparents living--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had the grandmother who lived in the same city. She lived with my uncle. My grandmother was blind. You know, she needed a lot of assistance. And I'm so happy now that it was I who mostly took care. Like, she needed to go to a doctor or to go to a mikve-- if you know what it means-- the ritual bath, which was so important. You didn't have a bath in your home. But this was a ritual, a Jewish ritual, that every woman had to go, once a month. And I would take my-- And since I was, like, the youngest of my three sis-- of us three, I would take, take her. And, of course, it was an ordeal. You know, I had things to do-- playing and whatever. But I loved my grandmother. And, and now I'm really happy that, that I could do it for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Heine Feige. And she, she was killed at age 72.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had the parental-- I had the fraternal-- my father's parents, who lived in Lutsk, in another city. And their name was Chaim and Feige. And we used to go visit them. Oh, that was such as thrill, I remember. It was wintertime. I don't know why I remember especially wintertime. My father would wake me up. It was pitch dark, and we would go take the choo-choo train, the train, the railway! It was exciting. I mean, there couldn't be a greater excitement in those days. And we went to visit his parents. And, and then he had some other relatives there. Evidently, he lived there before he got married to my mother because all the family was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to your home and the Jewish holidays. How were they celebrated in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Although my parents were kind of modern-- you know, because mostly-- with the beard, with the yarmulke, with-- you know, dressed traditionally-- my mother was very kosher. Not so much my father. But my mother was very kosher. And holidays, it was kosher and observed, and Shabbos was observed. Every Shabbos and every holiday. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides the cookies that you mentioned that were cooked every Thursday night, were there any other special foods that were prepared for Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, challah, challah, and there was gefilte fish. Without this, there wouldn't be a Shabbos. And, of course, chicken soup and, you know, and tsimmes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How about other Jewish holidays? Pesach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah-- yeah. There was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I do remember because we had to sit through for hours because, you know, you do it-- did it real traditionally. The whole Haggadah was, was read, you know. And we had to sit for hours. But then we would discuss. We will have discussions, which I say-- [SIGHS] maybe our household was a little not so traditional as the others. My sisters were very well-educated. And we would have discussions about the Bible-- like, Moses and all the, the Ten Commandments, how, how true it is, even though-- or the rituals, like the-- a Jew had to wash his hands before dinner. And, you know, in those days, the hygiene wasn't so observed like nowadays. You, you wash your hands 100 times a day. And, like, I remember my sister saying that even though-- even though it wasn't told by God, it's just the idea to how to-- how to keep the masses in order, how to prevail. Hygiene should prevail. If would be said even if it isn't. There's nobody to approve or disapprove. So it was very appropriate to tell them, yeah, God tells you to wash your hands before you sit down to eat, or stuff like this. We're like analyzing, which was really very interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who led the discussions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, everybody. But my oldest sister because she was the most-- you know, she was the brain-- the educator-- the educated girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who asked the four questions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had to. I was the youngest. Yeah. And everything was in Hebrew. I know nowadays you have the English versions, and everything is done in English. But, no, no, it was all Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other special Jewish holiday-- Purim, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, yeah. Purim was very important because we would dress up and have fun, you know? It was like Halloween.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any particular dress or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't have costumes like nowadays, the kids for Halloween, you know-- they buy or they make them up. I don't know, we dressed like-- I don't even remember. Anything we could put on to look funny.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about Sukkot? How did you celebrate that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautiful, beautiful. Because there was always a Sukkot, you know? And since not everyone in the neighborhood had one. So let's say the few houses-- and where we lived, it was all family. You know, it seemed like the whole clan.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was-- I remember very well, as if it would have been yesterday, you know. We went to the Sukkot. It was chilly. We'd have a little sweater over. And all the food was brought to the Sukkot. Oh, I liked it. I liked excitement. Excitement. What kind of food they serve-- did you have, in the sukkot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always traditional-- chicken soup. Without chicken soup, nothing beg-- began. No meal began-- I mean, an important meal. Chicken soup. Then we had beef flank and, you know. And, noodles, chicken noo-- chicken noodle soup, and beef, and a pickle, and, I don't know. I-- [LAUGHS] horseradish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the High Holy Days-- Yom Kippur and Rosh Hashanah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yom Kippur was very, very morbid. I mean, we didn't like it, you know? It wasn't fun. Yeah, it was very solemn. And, of course, as young as you were, you fasted all day. And the parents used to go to temple-- not to the big synagogue. My parents went-- My father was affiliated with that organization TOZ. And so I remember my parents went there. And the kids were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little more about that organization that you just mentioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was-- since my father was, you know, dealing with drugs and the doctors-- all in the med-- medical field, they established an institution-- or, I should say, an organization-- because people didn't know. They were ignorant as far as taking care of your health. You just thought you eat well-- as well as you can afford-- and that was enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then times became modern, and inoculations-- inoculations, you know, started taking place. So they created that-- it was called-- can I say it in Polish? Towarzystwo Ochrony Zdrowia, which means a Society for Health Care-- health care would be the right translation. So, whenever inoculation, you would go there. Or they would tell you how to take care of your health and whatever modern thing-- whatever modern method came out so to make people aware how to behave and what to do. And you were mentioning your father went to this--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father belonged.

speaker: interviewer
text: --place for Yom Kippur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And so they had their own-- as I said, there are all kinds of little-- sprang-- you know, branched out, because maybe people lived a little further. And nobody would take anything that was mobile on a holiday. So you had to reach it closer. You couldn't walk. Maybe you were old or sick or-- so you had, like, nearby, in a private-- maybe not a home, but-- yes-- specially designed for it. It was called a [? shtiebel. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do during Yom Kippur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I played. I fasted when I was very young. I fasted a half a day. And later on I fasted the whole day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What schools did you attend, as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I attended public school, which was seven years. And then the Russians occupied our, our part of Poland-- the Polish Ukraine. From '39 to '41, I went to a Russian school for two years until '41. Until Hitler attacked again and the war started. So I went to that middle school, which was all in Russian, you know, done.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any favorite subjects?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked religion. I was very good in it, you know? Maybe because it sounded like-- or mythology, you know? I guess it sounded like fairy tales. [LAUGHS] And, as I recall, music.
